message:
fix(net): add exponential backoff to transient accept errors and fallback IO rewakes

Problem:
When the HTTP/1.1 listener encounters transient accept errors (e.g.
EMFILE, ECONNABORTED), it immediately retries via `continue` with no
delay. Under sustained pressure — such as file-descriptor exhaustion or
a SYN-flood — this creates a tight busy-loop that saturates the CPU and
starves other tasks of runtime capacity, making recovery harder.

Similarly, in TcpStream, several fallback paths call
`cx.waker().wake_by_ref()` to re-poll immediately when no IO driver is
available or when the socket reports NotConnected/Unsupported. This
causes spin-polling at full speed, wasting CPU cycles and preventing
the scheduler from making forward progress on other work.

Changes:

src/http/h1/listener.rs
  - Add two constants TRANSIENT_ACCEPT_BACKOFF_BASE (2ms) and
    TRANSIENT_ACCEPT_BACKOFF_CAP (64ms) that define the boundaries
    of the backoff window.
  - Track consecutive transient accept failures with a
    `transient_accept_streak` counter in the accept loop. The counter
    resets to 0 on every successful accept and increments (with
    saturating_add) on each transient error.
  - Introduce `transient_accept_backoff_delay()` which computes a
    stepped exponential backoff: the delay doubles every 16 consecutive
    failures (exponent = (streak-1)/16, capped at 5), yielding steps
    of 2ms -> 4ms -> 8ms -> 16ms -> 32ms -> 64ms. The 16-failure
    plateau per step keeps the listener responsive under brief bursts
    while still ramping up to protect the system under prolonged load.
  - Expand `is_transient_accept_error()` to also classify WouldBlock
    and TimedOut as transient, since these are common under heavy load
    or non-blocking socket configurations and are equally recoverable.
  - Add unit tests: `transient_error_detection` now covers WouldBlock
    and TimedOut; a new `transient_backoff_caps` test validates the
    step function at key boundaries (streak 1, 16, 17, 10000).

src/net/tcp/stream.rs
  - Add a FALLBACK_IO_BACKOFF constant (1ms) to throttle fallback
    re-wake paths.
  - Extract a `fallback_rewake()` helper that sleeps for
    FALLBACK_IO_BACKOFF before calling `cx.waker().wake_by_ref()`.
    This replaces bare `wake_by_ref()` calls in five locations:
    the NotConnected branch of poll_shutdown, the two `None` guard
    returns (no Cx / no IO driver), the Unsupported error path in
    register_interest, and the NotConnected branch inside
    rearm_connect_registration.
  - The 1ms sleep is small enough to be imperceptible for real
    connections but eliminates spin-polling, dramatically reducing
    idle CPU usage when the runtime falls back to waker-driven polling
    without an epoll/kqueue driver.

Together these changes harden the networking layer against CPU waste
during transient failures and degraded-driver scenarios.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
